summary:
The Expedition of the Thousand (Spedizione dei Mille) was a military campaign led by Giuseppe Garibaldi in 1860–61 that conquered the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies and brought southern Italy into the emerging Italian national state. Garibaldi's remarkable achievement — conquering a kingdom of nine million people with a volunteer force of just over a thousand red-shirted fighters — was one of the most dramatic military episodes of the [[event:2533|Italian Unification]] process.

Garibaldi, a charismatic revolutionary who had fought for republican causes in South America and Italy, sailed from Quarto near Genoa in May 1860 with 1,089 volunteers aboard two steamships. Landing at Marsala in western Sicily, his force routed Bourbon armies vastly larger than itself through a combination of tactical surprise, popular support from Sicilian peasants and nationalists, and Garibaldi's personal magnetism. Palermo fell in June 1860, and within weeks the Bourbon position in Sicily had collapsed. Garibaldi crossed to the mainland in August and swept northward, entering Naples in September as the Bourbon king fled.

The success of the expedition confronted the Kingdom of Sardinia under Cavour with a dilemma: Garibaldi, a republican in sympathy, might attempt to march on Rome or establish a separate southern state. Cavour ordered Sardinian forces to invade the Papal States from the north, cutting off Garibaldi's northward advance and ensuring that the south would be incorporated into a monarchical rather than a republican Italy. Garibaldi, placing national unity above his personal republican principles, handed his conquests to the Sardinian king Victor Emmanuel II at their famous meeting near Naples in October 1860. The unified Kingdom of Italy was proclaimed in March 1861.
